quote: Well, we we had someone come and visit us, you know, just kinda going door to door talking about solar and said, oh, man. Maybe this is something to look into. Yeah. We've been we've looked a few years back about the with Ionic about buying, and that just wasn't the right fit for us. But this just looking at the different options of the different lease the PPAs, I think they're called, like, leases.
stewart_read: Jim handed Holland a rich emotional anchor: a prior solar evaluation that didn't fit, active curiosity about PPAs vs purchase, and a door-knocker as the trigger. The codex marks interest_in_solar as the emotional anchor of the entire pitch, with depth_grading: true and a note that top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Holland's response was 'Mhmm. Yeah.' followed immediately by the credit qualifier. This is a reactive drop, not a strategic deferral — probing Jim's prior Ionic experience and his PPA curiosity is squarely setter-tier work, not closer-tier content.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland was holding scope discipline by not engaging with product-level PPA/purchase distinctions. Rejected: the codex explicitly marks interest_in_solar as required with depth_grading: true. Asking 'what didn't fit about the Ionic experience?' is emotional anchoring, not product explanation.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the codex note: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Jim gave him a two-part story (prior bad fit + new curiosity). A single follow-up — 'What was it about the Ionic experience that didn't work out?' — would have deepened the anchor without scope creep. Manager should replay this moment and ask Holland what he heard Jim say.

quote: Yeah. I mean, with this company, we've we've looked and pulled up the you know, looked at our bills. I mean, it's up to $2.70 in the summer and down to, you know, $1.01 $1.50 in the winter.
stewart_read: Holland captured the bill range and made a partial flip attempt: 'I know that ComEd is they've been raising their rates quite a bit from what I've heard, so I'm glad you guys are looking into an alternative.' This is a partial activation — it names ComEd as adversary, which is the right instinct. But it landed as a passing comment ('from what I've heard') rather than a pattern interrupt that turns Jim's own number against the utility. The codex's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip'; Holland executed 'ask + acknowledge + soft comment.' The gap is real but not a pure drop.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's ComEd comment was sufficient emotional framing for a warm, already-motivated customer. Partially defensible — Jim didn't need much convincing. But the flip opportunity was still available and unused.
coaching_implication: The coaching point is calibration, not correction. Holland found the right instinct (name the utility as adversary) but didn't commit to it. A suggested intent: take Jim's own number ('$270 in the summer') and make it the villain — 'That $270 is going to ComEd every summer whether solar works or not. The question is whether it keeps going there.' Manager should use this as a flip-technique introduction, not a failure replay.

quote: We have a pretty good program right now, I'd say. So what it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar.
stewart_read: Holland made an ungrounded financial promise: a savings guarantee within the first year. The codex's numeric_discipline is explicit — reps may not invent, estimate, or imply savings not grounded in the call or schema. No bill analysis has been completed at this point in the call; the bill hasn't even been collected yet. This is a compliance gap that creates a closer-stage expectation the closer must either honor or walk back.
coaching_implication: This is a closer-stage liability, not just a coaching note. The closer walks in with Jim expecting a guaranteed savings figure. Manager should flag this as a promise-made item in the handoff and coach Holland on the distinction between 'our program is designed to save you money' (intent framing, no guarantee) and 'guarantees you savings within the first year' (a specific financial commitment Holland has no basis to make).

quote: Yeah, if your credit is above 670, you'll also qualify for any state incentives, as well. So I'll make sure you get those.
stewart_read: Holland made a blanket incentive guarantee — 'you'll also qualify for any state incentives' and 'I'll make sure you get those.' The codex explicitly notes the tax credit can no longer be guaranteed and that purchase options without the tax credit exist. A blanket state-incentives promise is not authorized by the schema and creates a specific expectation the closer must manage. This compounds the savings-guarantee promise made 8 seconds earlier.
coaching_implication: Two ungrounded financial promises in 15 seconds is a pattern, not a one-off. Manager should address both together in the 1-on-1: the issue isn't that Holland is wrong about incentives existing — it's that 'I'll make sure you get those' is a personal guarantee Holland cannot back. Suggested intent: 'There are state incentives available in Illinois — our specialist will walk you through exactly what you qualify for.' Routes the specifics to the closer without making a promise Holland can't keep.

quote: And does it need to be both of us, or is it just I mean, if I can do it, it's easier to do morning?
stewart_read: Jim asked directly whether both spouses need to be present. Holland's response — 'we like them both to be there, but I guess if your wife isn't on the title, then we could just do it with you' — gave Jim permission to exclude his wife. The codex is unambiguous: the correct response is to include the spouse, not to offer an out. The title-status uncertainty (Jim said 'maybe my wife' at 01:27) was the opening to say 'let's plan to have her on the call and the specialist can sort out the title question.' Instead, Holland used the uncertainty as a reason to let Jim off the hook.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland was correctly applying the codex's 'if your wife isn't on the title' exception. Rejected: the codex's protocols.spouse_decision says 'DO NOT try to overcome this. Include the spouse.' The title-status was uncertain, not confirmed absent. Holland resolved the uncertainty in the wrong direction.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-risk structural gap. The appointment is now booked with one decision-maker, and the wife's title status is unresolved. Manager should walk Holland through the codex's spouse protocol and the SESSION18_fd078269 positive example (Randy/Juan booked in 4 min by reframing the appointment as the spouse-inclusion venue). The coaching question for Holland: 'What would have happened if you'd said — let's make sure she's on the call, and the specialist will sort out the title question with you both?'

quote: And so just so you know, I just sent it back to you.
stewart_read: Jim sent the bill back to Holland unprompted during a scheduling pause — Holland hadn't even finished asking for a time slot. This is a strong enthusiasm signal: Jim is actively invested in moving forward, not passively compliant. Holland's response ('Oh, awesome. Thanks. That was so quick.') acknowledged it warmly but didn't anchor on it. A rep who recognized this signal could have used it to reinforce Jim's own motivation: 'The fact that you already had that ready tells me you've been thinking about this for a while.'
coaching_implication: Surface this for Holland as a positive signal he can learn to recognize and anchor. The manager doesn't need to frame this as a failure — Holland handled it warmly. The coaching point is: when a customer does something that reveals genuine investment, name it back to them. It deepens commitment before the appointment.

quote: K. Great. Well, I'll get you solidified, for Thursday at ten, and then I'll send you a text just with your appointment details so you can add it to your calendar as well.
stewart_read: Holland completed the appointment confirmation and text-followup promise but skipped the 'Is that fair?' verbal handshake — a required button_up element the codex marks as 'Predictive of show rate. Don't skip it.' The call ended cleanly but without the verbal commitment that anchors the appointment in Jim's mind as a mutual agreement rather than a scheduled event.
alternative_hypothesis: On a warm, cooperative customer who proactively sent the bill, the verbal handshake may be lower-stakes than on a skeptical customer. Partially defensible — Jim's cooperation signals genuine intent. But the codex marks this as required regardless of customer warmth, and the spouse-exclusion makes this appointment more fragile than it appears.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the spouse-handling gap in the 1-on-1: the appointment has two structural vulnerabilities (wife excluded, no verbal handshake). Neither is fatal on a warm customer, but together they reduce show-rate confidence. The 'Is that fair?' ask takes three seconds and costs nothing. Manager should make this a habit-formation note, not a failure replay.

quote: Yeah. We just had them put on this last in '25. Oh, nice. Okay. So Yeah. Just re reeled. Yep. Yep. Had some some hail damage, so got it redone.
stewart_read: Jim disclosed a brand-new roof (2025, hail damage replacement) — this is a strong solar-readiness signal. A new roof eliminates the most common solar-install complication and removes the re-roof objection entirely. Holland acknowledged it warmly ('That's too bad though about the hail, but then you got the place') but didn't anchor on it as a qualifier. The correct move is to name it as a positive: 'A new roof actually makes the install cleaner — no re-roof needed before we put panels up.'
coaching_implication: This is a low-stakes coaching point on a call that booked, but it's a good habit-formation example. When a customer discloses a new roof, name it as a qualifier out loud — it reinforces the customer's sense that their home is a good fit and builds momentum toward the appointment. Manager can use this as a positive example of what to anchor on, not a failure to correct.
